new_options.rationale: Tocolysis is contraindicated in abruption with maternal compromise and foetal distress; immediate delivery is needed (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Insert two large-bore IV cannulae, give 2 L crystalloid bolus and reassess in 30 minutes
new_options.rationale: Resuscitation occurs concurrently with delivery preparation, not as an isolated step; delaying delivery worsens outcomes for both (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Attempt artificial rupture of membranes to expedite vaginal delivery
new_options.rationale: Vaginal birth may be considered for abruption only when mother and foetus are stable and labour is advanced; with shock and bradycardia, caesarean is required (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Transfer to tertiary centre for higher-level obstetric input
new_options.rationale: She cannot be transferred safely; on-site immediate delivery is required (RANZCOG).

verdicts.discard_reason: Substantively duplicates obgyn-batch-022 (major praevia at late preterm) and obgyn-batch-030 (already discarded). One praevia question is sufficient in the bank.

verdicts.new_stem: A 24-year-old G1P0 at 8+2 weeks gestation presents with vaginal bleeding and lower abdominal cramps. Speculum: cervical os is open with a small amount of tissue at the os. TVUS: empty uterus, endometrial thickness 8 mm, no retained products clearly visible, no adnexal mass, no free fluid. Beta-hCG 1200 IU/L today (was 8500 IU/L two days ago). She is haemodynamically stable, Hb 122, blood group A Rh-positive, no allergies. She has had no prior surgery and is keen to be home with her partner. The team is choosing between expectant, medical and surgical management.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Offer expectant, medical (misoprostol) or surgical (suction curettage) management options, and support her choice
new_options.rationale: This is an incomplete or completing miscarriage in a haemodynamically stable woman. Australian guidance (RANZCOG, eTG) supports shared decision-making between expectant, medical and surgical management with comparable safety; choice depends on patient preference, bleeding and availability.

new_options.text: Proceed directly to suction curettage in theatre today
new_options.rationale: Surgical management is one option but not mandatory; expectant and medical are equally valid in a stable patient (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Administer single-dose methotrexate as the pregnancy may be ectopic
new_options.rationale: TVUS confirms intrauterine pregnancy products and beta-hCG is falling appropriately; ectopic management is not indicated (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Admit for IV oxytocin infusion to expedite expulsion of retained products
new_options.rationale: Oxytocin is not standard for first-trimester miscarriage; misoprostol is the medical option (eTG).

new_options.text: Discharge home with no follow-up; the falling beta-hCG indicates resolution
new_options.rationale: Follow-up beta-hCG to confirm trend to negative and a follow-up review are required to exclude retained products or molar pregnancy (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 32-year-old primigravida with diet-controlled GDM and an EFW of 4.5 kg has just had the foetal head deliver with retraction and turtle-neck sign. McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure (30 seconds), attempted delivery of the posterior arm (twice), and internal rotation manoeuvres (Rubin II and Woods' screw) have all failed. Time from delivery of the head: 4 minutes. CTG showed normal trace before delivery. The woman has an epidural in situ. The midwife asks the senior registrar if firm fundal pressure should now be tried while preparing for the next manoeuvre.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Roll the woman onto hands and knees (Gaskin all-fours manoeuvre) and reattempt delivery of the posterior arm
new_options.rationale: After failure of McRoberts, suprapubic pressure, posterior arm and internal rotation, the next recommended step in the RANZCOG algorithm is the all-fours (Gaskin) manoeuvre. Fundal pressure is contraindicated as it worsens impaction and risks uterine rupture and OASIS (RANZCOG shoulder dystocia).

new_options.text: Apply firm fundal pressure to dislodge the impacted shoulder
new_options.rationale: Fundal pressure is a classic anti-pattern in shoulder dystocia; it worsens impaction, risks uterine rupture and increases brachial plexus injury (RANZCOG; RCOG).

new_options.text: Perform symphysiotomy to enlarge the pelvic outlet
new_options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is a last-resort manoeuvre in settings where caesarean is unavailable; in an Australian hospital it precedes only Zavanelli and only after all-fours and other manoeuvres have failed (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Perform Zavanelli cephalic replacement and proceed to caesarean section
new_options.rationale: Zavanelli is the absolute last resort after all other manoeuvres have failed, including all-fours; it is associated with high foetal mortality (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Repeat McRoberts and suprapubic pressure for another 60 seconds
new_options.rationale: Repeating already-failed manoeuvres wastes time; the algorithm proceeds to all-fours after the first internal manoeuvre sequence has failed (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 31-year-old woman with primary obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome (three consecutive first-trimester miscarriages; persistent lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin IgG and anti-beta2GPI IgG on testing 12 weeks apart, no history of thromboembolic event) is now 6 weeks pregnant. She has been on warfarin 5 mg daily for the past 9 months as 'prevention' prescribed by another clinician. She has had no thrombosis. She has no other medical conditions and no allergies.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate medication regimen for this pregnancy?

new_options.text: Continue warfarin 5 mg daily throughout pregnancy as she is already established on it
new_options.rationale: Warfarin is teratogenic between 6 and 12 weeks (warfarin embryopathy: nasal hypoplasia, stippled epiphyses) and is contraindicated in obstetric APS without prior thrombosis. This is a critical anti-pattern (RANZCOG; eTG).

new_options.text: Stop warfarin immediately and commence low-dose aspirin 100 mg daily plus prophylactic-dose enoxaparin 40 mg subcutaneously daily, continuing through pregnancy and for 6 weeks postpartum
new_options.rationale: Obstetric APS (recurrent miscarriage, no prior thrombosis) is treated with low-dose aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH. Warfarin must be ceased before 6 weeks to avoid embryopathy; it is replaced by LMWH (RANZCOG; eTG).

new_options.text: Continue warfarin until 12 weeks then switch to LMWH
new_options.rationale: Warfarin embryopathy risk is concentrated in weeks 6-12; continuing through this window is unsafe (RANZCOG; eTG).

new_options.text: Continue warfarin until 6 weeks then aspirin alone for the rest of the pregnancy
new_options.rationale: Aspirin alone is inferior to aspirin + LMWH in obstetric APS with recurrent loss; LMWH should be added (RANZCOG; eTG).

new_options.text: Stop warfarin and commence intravenous immunoglobulin monthly as primary therapy
new_options.rationale: IVIG is not first-line for obstetric APS; aspirin + LMWH is. IVIG is reserved for refractory cases on clinical trial protocols (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 26-year-old primigravida at 38 weeks with PROM 28 hours ago develops T 39.2, HR 124, RR 28, BP 88/52 (MAP 64), confused with GCS 14. Tender uterus, purulent vaginal discharge, foetal HR 175 bpm with reduced variability. Lactate 4.6, WCC 19. IV access obtained, bloods sent, blood cultures taken. The on-call team has called for theatre.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Administer IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin + metronidazole) and a 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid bolus within the next hour, while preparing for emergency delivery
new_options.rationale: The Sepsis Six / Surviving Sepsis Hour-1 bundle includes blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, lactate, fluid resuscitation 30 mL/kg, oxygen and source control. In chorioamnionitis with maternal sepsis and foetal compromise, delivery is the source control and is performed in parallel with resuscitation (eTG; RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Transfer to ICU for central venous access before commencing antibiotics
new_options.rationale: Antibiotics within 1 hour are the priority; CVC insertion must not delay them (Surviving Sepsis).

new_options.text: Proceed immediately to caesarean section as the primary intervention before fluids or antibiotics
new_options.rationale: Antibiotics and fluids are given concurrently with theatre preparation; antibiotics should not be delayed for surgery (Surviving Sepsis; RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Administer IV paracetamol 1 g and reassess vital signs in 30 minutes before further intervention
new_options.rationale: Temperature reduction does not treat sepsis; immediate antibiotics, fluids and source control are required (Surviving Sepsis).

new_options.text: Order CT abdomen and pelvis to identify the source before starting antibiotics
new_options.rationale: Source is clinically obvious (chorioamnionitis); CT delays antibiotics and is not safe in this critically unwell pregnant patient (Surviving Sepsis).

verdicts.new_stem: A 34-year-old G3P2 at 39+2 in spontaneous labour at 5 cm dilatation has a history of one lower-segment caesarean and one classical caesarean 1 year ago for preterm breech delivery. The midwifery team had counselled her against TOLAC due to the classical scar, but she insisted on attempting vaginal birth. Over the last 15 minutes she has developed constant severe suprapubic pain not relieved between contractions; the CTG now shows prolonged bradycardia 70 bpm; the foetal parts are easily felt abdominally, the uterine fundus is not palpable, and on VE the presenting part has receded from -1 to -3. Maternal HR 130, BP 88/52.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Activate the massive transfusion protocol and proceed to immediate category 1 caesarean section with anticipated laparotomy and possible hysterectomy
new_options.rationale: This is uterine rupture (very high risk with a prior classical scar in labour). Immediate operative delivery with MTP activation and senior consultant input is required; vaginal delivery, augmentation, tocolysis and further confirmatory tests are all unsafe (RANZCOG VBAC; RCOG).

new_options.text: Commence IV oxytocin to augment contractions and expedite vaginal delivery
new_options.rationale: Augmentation in suspected rupture worsens haemorrhage and foetal compromise; this is a critical anti-pattern (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Apply fundal pressure to expedite vaginal delivery as the head was previously at -1
new_options.rationale: Fundal pressure in suspected rupture causes catastrophic extension and is contraindicated (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Administer subcutaneous terbutaline 250 mcg tocolysis and reassess in 10 minutes
new_options.rationale: Tocolysis is for foetal distress in intact uterus with manageable cause; in rupture, delaying delivery worsens outcomes (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Perform speculum examination and foetal fibronectin to confirm membrane rupture before surgery
new_options.rationale: Confirmation is clinical and intraoperative; further bedside testing delays surgery (RANZCOG).

verdicts.discard_reason: Foetal arrhythmia transplacental therapy is a tertiary-MFM topic beyond end-of-MD Australian curriculum expectations; the bank prioritises common end-of-MD scenarios per high-yield focus rule.

verdicts.new_stem: A 30-year-old G3P0 at 21+5 weeks presents with pelvic pressure and a bulging sensation. Speculum: cervix dilated 3 cm with exposed amniotic membranes visible at the os. No fever, no uterine tenderness; WCC 10.2, CRP 8, vaginal swab GBS negative, foetal fibronectin not applicable, no pooling of liquor. Ultrasound: viable foetus, cervical length 0 mm with funnelling, normal AFI, no foetal anomalies. Neonatology has counselled the woman and she wants every appropriate intervention to prolong the pregnancy.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate combination of management steps?

new_options.text: Offer emergency McDonald cerclage, short-course indomethacin tocolysis (48 hours) given gestation under 32 weeks, antibiotic prophylaxis, and antenatal corticosteroids when delivery becomes likely
new_options.rationale: Australian/RANZCOG practice supports emergency rescue cerclage at 22-23+6 weeks for advanced cervical dilation with intact membranes and no infection, combined with short-course indomethacin tocolysis (limited to <32 weeks because of ductal closure risk above that gestation), antibiotic prophylaxis, and ACS when delivery is anticipated (RANZCOG cerclage; RCOG GT60).

new_options.text: Cerclage is contraindicated with bulging membranes; give only bed rest and corticosteroids
new_options.rationale: Bulging membranes are not an absolute contraindication; rescue cerclage may be considered if no infection or labour; bed rest alone has poor outcomes (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Place cerclage and give corticosteroids but avoid indomethacin entirely due to ductal closure risk
new_options.rationale: Ductal closure risk applies primarily after 32 weeks; at 21+5 short-course indomethacin is acceptable and useful (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Induce labour now as 21+5 is non-viable and ongoing pregnancy is futile
new_options.rationale: At 22+0 weeks active resuscitation is increasingly offered in Australia; the woman wants intervention and her foetus is approaching the threshold (ANZNN periviable consensus).

new_options.text: Place an abdominal cervicoisthmic cerclage today and commence IV magnesium sulfate
new_options.rationale: Abdominal cerclage is reserved for failed transvaginal cerclage in prior pregnancies, not for first rescue; MgSO4 is for neuroprotection from 24+0 weeks (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 37-year-old G5P4 with four prior lower-segment caesarean sections has anterior major placenta praevia. Ultrasound and MRI at 30 weeks confirm placenta accreta (loss of retroplacental clear zone, lacunae, hypervascularity at uterovesical interface), without percreta or bladder invasion. Foetal growth and Dopplers are normal. She has completed a course of betamethasone. She has been counselled about haemorrhage and hysterectomy and accepts a planned caesarean hysterectomy. The MDT is finalising timing and team composition.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate delivery plan?

new_options.text: Planned caesarean hysterectomy at 34-35+6 weeks at a tertiary centre by a multidisciplinary team (obstetrician, gynae-oncology, interventional radiology, anaesthesia, haematology, neonatology), with cell salvage and MTP on standby and a vertical/midline abdominal incision
new_options.rationale: RANZCOG and SMFM consensus recommend planned delivery at 34-35+6 weeks for placenta accreta spectrum (before frequent late-preterm bleeding risk) in a tertiary centre with full multidisciplinary team and blood-product support. The placenta is generally left in situ during hysterectomy (RANZCOG PAS; SMFM).

new_options.text: Elective caesarean at 39 weeks under a single general obstetrician at her local hospital
new_options.rationale: Awaiting 39 weeks risks emergency haemorrhage in the late preterm window; PAS requires planned earlier delivery and tertiary-centre MDT (RANZCOG; SMFM).

new_options.text: Plan vaginal delivery with active third-stage management and theatre on standby
new_options.rationale: Vaginal delivery is contraindicated in placenta praevia with PAS; caesarean is mandatory (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Plan delivery at 32 weeks to maximise prevention of any spontaneous labour
new_options.rationale: 32 weeks is too early without acute indication; the prematurity risk to the neonate outweighs the small additional protection against labour onset (RANZCOG; SMFM).

new_options.text: Admit at 34 weeks for inpatient monitoring and deliver only when spontaneous labour or distress occurs
new_options.rationale: PAS requires planned, not reactive, delivery to optimise team availability and reduce haemorrhage mortality (RANZCOG).

verdicts.discard_reason: Primary dysmenorrhoea first-line therapy is already covered in revised obgyn-batch-036; a second primary-dysmenorrhoea question adds no bank value.
BV diagnosis is already covered in obgyn-sh-001 (now revised to recurrent-BV management); pure Amsel-criteria diagnostic recall does not add bank value at L3.

verdicts.new_stem: A 68-year-old G4P4 woman presents with 6 months of a vaginal bulge worse at end of day. She also has bothersome stress urinary incontinence on coughing/sneezing and needs to splint to void fully. No faecal incontinence, no dyspareunia. Sexually active with husband, BMI 27, hypertension on perindopril, no other comorbidities. POP-Q: Ba 0, C -2, Bp -3 (stage 2 anterior compartment). Post-void residual 80 mL. Urodynamic studies confirm urodynamic stress incontinence with no detrusor overactivity and good bladder capacity. She wants the option with the best evidence for both prolapse and urinary symptoms; she is prepared to consider surgery but would also consider a pessary.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

new_options.text: Trial vaginal ring pessary and supervised pelvic floor physiotherapy, with reassessment of urinary symptoms after prolapse reduction
new_options.rationale: Conservative management (pessary + pelvic floor physiotherapy) is first-line for symptomatic POP including in women with concurrent stress incontinence. Pessary may unmask or improve SUI; reassessment after reduction guides whether surgery for incontinence is needed (RANZCOG; IUGA).

new_options.text: Anterior colporrhaphy with concomitant retropubic midurethral sling
new_options.rationale: Mesh midurethral sling implantation has been restricted by the TGA in Australia following safety concerns; combined surgery is not first-line and conservative options should be exhausted first (RANZCOG; TGA).

new_options.text: Sacrocolpopexy with synthetic mesh under general anaesthesia
new_options.rationale: Abdominal mesh sacrocolpopexy is reserved for apical/post-hysterectomy prolapse, not first-line for stage-2 anterior compartment; restricted in Australia (TGA mesh).

new_options.text: Indwelling urinary catheter to manage the elevated post-void residual long-term
new_options.rationale: PVR of 80 mL is mildly raised and likely improves with prolapse reduction; long-term catheter risks infection and is not appropriate (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Topical oestrogen as monotherapy with no other intervention
new_options.rationale: Vaginal oestrogen is an adjunct to pessary tolerance but does not treat prolapse or SUI alone (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 42-year-old G2P2 woman presents with 4 months of postcoital bleeding, small-volume bright red, after most episodes of intercourse. No intermenstrual bleeding, no abnormal discharge, no pelvic pain. Cycles regular, COCP for 5 years. Her last cervical screening test 9 months ago: HPV non-16/18 detected with reflex LBC showing pLSIL; she did not attend her recommended 12-month repeat. On speculum today: a 1.5 cm fungating lesion on the anterior cervical lip that bleeds on contact, with surrounding pale tissue. No cervical motion tenderness, no adnexal mass on bimanual examination.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Urgent referral to gynaecological oncology for biopsy of the cervical lesion
new_options.rationale: A visible suspicious cervical lesion overrides any cytology pathway; the cervical screening program states a clinically suspicious cervix must be referred for biopsy by a gynaecologist (preferably gynae-oncology). Repeat HPV testing is not appropriate (NCSP; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

new_options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test in clinic today
new_options.rationale: Screening is for asymptomatic women without visible lesions; a fungating lesion mandates biopsy not re-screen (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

new_options.text: Stop the COCP and review in 3 months as it may be the cause of bleeding
new_options.rationale: Postcoital bleeding with a suspicious cervical lesion is not attributable to COCP; this delays cancer diagnosis (NCSP; RANZCOG).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

new_options.text: Take chlamydia and gonorrhoea NAAT swabs and treat empirically while awaiting results
new_options.rationale: STI screen is reasonable but does not replace urgent biopsy of a visible suspicious lesion (NCSP; eTG).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

new_options.text: Reassure and arrange clinical review in 6 months
new_options.rationale: Reassurance is unsafe given the visible lesion and missed previous follow-up; cancer must be excluded (NCSP).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia: National Cervical Screening Program clinical guidelines

verdicts.new_stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 attends her 28-week antenatal visit. Her partner attends every visit, answers questions for her, and asked the midwife last visit to keep him informed of all her results. Today she is quiet, has a fading bruise on her left forearm she attributes to a kitchen accident, and SFH/foetal heart are appropriate. The clinic has a private room, an interpreter line and an on-site social worker. The midwife steps out and asks the GP registrar what to do next; the partner is in the room with her.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step to enable safe domestic violence enquiry?

new_options.text: Ask the screening questions in front of the partner to demonstrate transparency and gather his perspective
new_options.rationale: Asking in the presence of the suspected perpetrator places the woman at increased risk of escalation and produces unreliable answers; this is a critical anti-pattern (RACGP IPV; WHO).
source_refs: RACGP: Abuse and violence - working with our patients

new_options.text: Find a clinical reason to see her alone (e.g. urine sample, listen to baby in another room) and enquire privately using a validated framework such as HARK or RADAR
new_options.rationale: Routine antenatal IPV enquiry must occur in private without the partner; a face-saving clinical reason to separate them, in an environment with social-work backup, is the recommended approach (RACGP; WHO).
source_refs: RACGP: Abuse and violence - working with our patients

new_options.text: Defer the enquiry to the postnatal home visit, where the partner is less likely to attend
new_options.rationale: Antenatal enquiry is recommended in part because pregnancy is a high-risk period for IPV escalation; deferring to postnatal misses the opportunity to support her now (RACGP).
source_refs: RACGP: Abuse and violence - working with our patients

new_options.text: Avoid direct enquiry today and place a 1800RESPECT leaflet in her hand-held antenatal record for her to see
new_options.rationale: Visible leaflets in shared records are unsafe if the partner reviews them; safe enquiry in private is recommended (RACGP).
source_refs: RACGP: Abuse and violence - working with our patients

new_options.text: Contact NSW Police immediately because the forearm bruise constitutes evidence of an assault
new_options.rationale: Mandatory police reporting against the woman's wishes can endanger her and damage the therapeutic alliance; she must consent to any external action (RACGP; WHO).
source_refs: RACGP: Abuse and violence - working with our patients

verdicts.new_stem: A 22-year-old G1P0 Aboriginal woman from a regional NSW community books for antenatal care at 11 weeks. She has no medical conditions, BMI 24, supportive partner, lives with her mother and cousins. She tells the booking midwife she felt unsafe at a previous gynaecology appointment because she did not feel listened to and had to bring her own information; she has missed two prior follow-up appointments at other services for the same reason. The hospital offers: standard midwifery group practice (MGP); a local Aboriginal Maternal and Infant Health Service (AMIHS) co-led by an Aboriginal Health Worker and continuity midwife linked with the regional ACCHO; obstetrician-led clinic; GP shared care; and telehealth. All five pathways can access morphology USS, OGTT and specialist obstetric review.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which model of care is most likely to optimise engagement and outcomes for this woman?

new_options.text: AMIHS continuity-of-carer model integrating midwife and Aboriginal Health Worker, with ACCHO linkage
new_options.rationale: Birthing-on-Country and AMIHS-type continuity-of-carer models have evidence for improved engagement, reduced preterm birth and improved cultural safety for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander women. Given her history of disengagement from non-culturally-safe services, this is the best option (RANZCOG; AHMAC Birthing on Country).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Care of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Women

new_options.text: Standard midwifery group practice with referral to a separate Aboriginal Health Worker as needed
new_options.rationale: Bolting on an AHW as a referral is less effective than an integrated model where the AHW and midwife share continuity from the start (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Care of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Women

new_options.text: Obstetrician-led care because all Aboriginal pregnancies are inherently high-risk and require specialist supervision
new_options.rationale: Generalising all Aboriginal pregnancies as high-risk is racist and contradicts evidence; risk should be individually assessed (RANZCOG; AHMAC).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Care of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Women

new_options.text: GP shared care with the regional GP to minimise hospital contact
new_options.rationale: GP shared care is a valid model but does not provide the integrated cultural and clinical continuity that AMIHS offers for this woman (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Care of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Women

new_options.text: Telehealth-only antenatal care to reduce her travel burden regardless of her preference
new_options.rationale: Telehealth is an adjunct, not a substitute for face-to-face culturally safe care, and removing in-person AHW/midwife contact reduces engagement (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Care of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Women

verdicts.new_stem: A 26-year-old woman disclosed intimate partner violence 4 weeks ago and you drew up a written safety plan (code words, packed bag at her sister's, 1800RESPECT number). Today she reports her partner has been kind, brought flowers, and agreed to attend couples counselling; she is reconsidering whether she still needs the plan. No acute injuries. The previous incident involved choking, which she had not initially disclosed. She is calm, oriented, has no children, joint rental, independent income, family interstate. She asks whether the change means the cycle of abuse is over and whether joint counselling would now be safe.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate response?

new_options.text: Validate her experience, explain the reconciliation/honeymoon phase of the cycle, advise against joint couples counselling (which is unsafe in active IPV with prior strangulation), reinforce the safety plan and offer ongoing review
new_options.rationale: Joint couples counselling is contraindicated in active IPV (especially with non-fatal strangulation, a major homicide risk marker) because it places the victim at risk and reinforces coercive control. Validation, education about the cycle and safety planning are the recommended steps (RACGP; WHO; ANROWS).

new_options.text: Reassure her that the apology phase usually means violence will not recur and discard the safety plan
new_options.rationale: The honeymoon phase predictably gives way to renewed tension and incident phases; reassurance and abandoning the plan is unsafe (RACGP; WHO).

new_options.text: Insist she leave the relationship today as the only safe option
new_options.rationale: Leaving is the highest-risk time for homicide; her autonomy and timing must be respected with safety planning (ANROWS).

new_options.text: Recommend joint couples counselling because the partner has agreed to attend
new_options.rationale: Joint counselling is contraindicated in active IPV; it provides the perpetrator a platform and worsens risk (WHO; RACGP).

new_options.text: Discharge her from clinical follow-up because she now feels safe
new_options.rationale: Ongoing follow-up is recommended through cycles; discharging removes the support network at the highest-risk time (RACGP).

verdicts.new_stem: A 25-year-old G1P0 woman in Perth, Western Australia presents at 24+2 weeks gestation by ultrasound requesting termination of pregnancy. She has no medical comorbidities and no foetal anomaly. The pregnancy is unwanted but she had previously been undecided. She is certain of her decision now and asks her GP to refer her. The GP knows the new WA Public Health Amendment (Abortion) Act 2023 was enacted in early 2024 with different rules above and below 23 weeks.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which statement most accurately reflects the legal pathway for her TOP in WA?

new_options.text: Lawful on request up to 23 weeks with one medical practitioner; at her current gestation (24+2) she needs assessment and agreement by two medical practitioners, at least one being an obstetrician/gynaecologist or in consultation with one
new_options.rationale: Under the WA Public Health Amendment (Abortion) Act 2023, TOP is lawful on request up to 23 weeks (with one practitioner), and after 23 weeks requires assessment by two medical practitioners with specialist input. Partner consent is not required (WA Department of Health; SHQ WA).

new_options.text: Unlawful in WA at this gestation under any circumstances; must travel interstate
new_options.rationale: TOP after 23 weeks is lawful in WA with two-practitioner assessment; interstate travel is not required (WA Act 2023).

new_options.text: Partner consent is required before the procedure
new_options.rationale: No Australian state or territory requires partner consent for TOP (WA Act 2023; equivalent state legislation).

new_options.text: A mandatory 7-day waiting period applies from first consultation to procedure
new_options.rationale: WA's 2023 Act removed the previous mandatory waiting period and counselling requirement that existed under the prior law (WA Act 2023; SHQ WA).

new_options.text: Must be performed surgically; medical TOP is not available in WA
new_options.rationale: Both medical and surgical TOP are available in WA; the choice depends on gestation and clinical factors (SHQ WA).

verdicts.new_stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 has her first ultrasound at 9+4 by certain LMP, showing two viable intrauterine pregnancies. The report describes two gestational sacs, two yolk sacs, a thick echogenic inter-twin membrane and a wedge of placental tissue extending into the base of the membrane (lambda sign). Both crown-rump lengths are concordant. The next appointment with maternal-foetal medicine is in 5 weeks. The community midwife asks the registrar what additional management is required as a direct consequence of this chorionicity assignment, beyond singleton routine care.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which management plan correctly reflects the chorionicity and the implications for care?

new_options.text: Dichorionic diamniotic twins: scan every 4 weeks from 24 weeks for growth and Dopplers, plan delivery by 37+6 weeks if uncomplicated
new_options.rationale: The lambda sign confirms dichorionic-diamniotic twins. Australian/RANZCOG guidance recommends 4-weekly growth scans from 24 weeks, delivery by 37+6 if uncomplicated (vs 36+0 for MCDA and 32-34 for MCMA). No 2-weekly TTTS surveillance is required (RANZCOG twins; ISUOG).

new_options.text: Monochorionic diamniotic twins: scan every 2 weeks from 16 weeks for TTTS, deliver by 36+6 weeks
new_options.rationale: The lambda sign indicates dichorionic; T-sign would indicate monochorionic-diamniotic. TTTS surveillance is not required (RANZCOG; ISUOG).

new_options.text: Monochorionic monoamniotic twins: admit from 24 weeks for daily CTG and deliver by 32-34 weeks
new_options.rationale: MCMA requires single sac and no inter-twin membrane; here there are two sacs and a thick membrane (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Chorionicity cannot be assigned until 11-14 weeks gestation
new_options.rationale: Chorionicity is best assigned in the first trimester (before 14 weeks); at 9+4 the lambda/T-sign is highly accurate (ISUOG).

new_options.text: Dichorionic monoamniotic twins exist and require shared monitoring; plan delivery by 34 weeks
new_options.rationale: Dichorionic monoamniotic twinning is not a genuine entity; each chorion has its own amnion (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 with a confirmed MCDA twin pregnancy at 19+5 weeks attends fortnightly surveillance. The 16-week scan was unremarkable. Today's ultrasound: twin 1 (donor) EFW 22nd centile, DVP 1.6 cm, bladder not seen on three sweeps over 30 minutes; twin 2 (recipient) EFW 56th centile, DVP 9.2 cm, distended bladder, normal MCA-PSV. Umbilical artery Dopplers normal both twins, no foetal hydrops, cervix 38 mm. She has no contractions, no bleeding, no symptoms of pre-eclampsia. The tertiary foetal medicine unit is 2 hours away and offers fetoscopic laser ablation daily. She wants the option that maximises the chance of two healthy survivors.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Urgent referral to a tertiary foetal medicine unit for fetoscopic laser ablation of the placental anastomoses
new_options.rationale: She has stage I/II TTTS (Quintero stage II given absent donor bladder, with oligohydramnios <2 cm vs polyhydramnios >8 cm; normal Dopplers excluding stage III; no hydrops). Fetoscopic laser ablation between 16-26 weeks gives the best dual-survival outcome and is recommended over amnioreduction (RANZCOG twins; ISUOG).

new_options.text: Reassure and continue routine fortnightly surveillance for 2 more weeks
new_options.rationale: Stage II TTTS without intervention has high mortality; awaiting progression is unsafe (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Amnioreduction of the recipient sac in the local maternity unit
new_options.rationale: Amnioreduction is symptomatic only; laser ablation has superior dual-survival outcomes and is the recommended first-line definitive treatment at 16-26 weeks (RANZCOG; ISUOG).

new_options.text: Admit for antenatal corticosteroids and plan delivery this week
new_options.rationale: Delivery at 19+5 is non-viable; pregnancy must be prolonged with definitive treatment (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Selective foetal reduction of the donor twin by cord occlusion now
new_options.rationale: Selective reduction is reserved for stage IV-V TTTS or where laser is not feasible; at stage II laser is preferred (RANZCOG; ISUOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 RhD-negative woman with a DCDA twin pregnancy (two viable embryos at 7+1) re-presents at 13+0 with a single episode of pink spotting yesterday, now resolved. She is haemodynamically stable. Ultrasound today: twin A viable, CRL appropriate for 13+0; twin B sac collapsed, embryo CRL 7+5 with no cardiac activity (demised). Antibody screen at booking and today: negative. She has not previously received anti-D in this pregnancy. She is otherwise well.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of management is most appropriate?

new_options.text: Reassure regarding minimal increased risk to the surviving twin from the early loss; administer anti-D 625 IU IM as the bleeding episode is at 13+0; continue routine DCDA twin surveillance
new_options.rationale: Early vanishing twin (before 14 weeks) confers minimal increased risk to the surviving co-twin. As she is now 13+0 with a sensitising event, anti-D 625 IU IM is required (NBA gives 625 IU from 13 weeks). DCDA surveillance continues 4-weekly from 24 weeks (NBA; RANZCOG).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

new_options.text: Administer anti-D 250 IU IM as the dose for sensitising events before 20 weeks
new_options.rationale: From 13 weeks the dose is 625 IU IM, not 250 IU; 250 IU is the pre-12-week dose (NBA).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

new_options.text: Withhold anti-D until 28 weeks routine prophylaxis as the antibody screen is negative
new_options.rationale: Sensitising events require anti-D within 72 hours regardless of antibody screen and irrespective of upcoming routine prophylaxis (NBA).
source_refs: National Blood Authority: Anti-D Ig guidelines

new_options.text: Surgical evacuation of the demised gestational sac to prevent later miscarriage of the surviving twin
new_options.rationale: Vanishing twin resorbs naturally and does not threaten the surviving twin; surgical intervention risks loss of the live twin (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Admit for serial coagulation studies given the increased maternal risk of disseminated intravascular coagulation
new_options.rationale: DIC risk applies to single-twin demise after viability (>=14-20 weeks) with prolonged retention; first-trimester vanishing twin does not carry this risk (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 39+3 presents 2 hours after a gush of clear fluid followed by ongoing leakage. Not contracting; speculum confirms a pool of clear amniotic fluid and a closed soft cervix. CTG reactive. GBS swab at 36 weeks was POSITIVE. She has no penicillin allergy. Booking BMI 24, otherwise uncomplicated pregnancy. She asks about waiting at home for spontaneous labour; she has no childcare for her older child and would prefer to wait if it is safe.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan in this Australian setting?

new_options.text: Admit for immediate induction of labour and commence intrapartum IV benzylpenicillin GBS prophylaxis now
new_options.rationale: Term PROM with GBS-positive status: RANZCOG recommends immediate induction and commencement of intrapartum GBS prophylaxis (rather than expectant management) to reduce risk of early-onset neonatal GBS sepsis. Penicillin is first-line in non-allergic women (RANZCOG term PROM; RANZCOG GBS).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

new_options.text: Admit for expectant management up to 24 hours with no antibiotics unless fever develops
new_options.rationale: GBS-positive women warrant immediate IAP regardless of expectant approach; delaying antibiotics increases neonatal sepsis risk (RANZCOG GBS).

new_options.text: Wait at home for spontaneous labour for up to 5 days with daily review
new_options.rationale: Outpatient expectant management for >24 hours in term PROM increases chorioamnionitis risk; GBS-positive status makes this clearly unsafe (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

new_options.text: Mandatory immediate lower-segment caesarean to prevent ascending infection
new_options.rationale: Vaginal birth is appropriate with IAP; caesarean is not indicated solely for GBS-positive PROM (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

new_options.text: Commence oral erythromycin to prolong latency to delivery at term
new_options.rationale: Latency antibiotics (erythromycin) are for preterm PROM, not term PROM; at term, induction is the goal (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

verdicts.new_stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 40+1 in spontaneous labour, admitted 6 hours ago at 5 cm, is re-examined and is now 6 cm at +0 station in direct OP position with mild caput and no moulding. Membranes ruptured spontaneously 4 hours ago with clear liquor. Contractions: palpate 3 in 10 lasting 40 seconds, mild intensity. CTG normal. Hb 124. Her mother is reminding the registrar that her own labour had to be 'started with the drip' and asks them to do the same.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Commence IV oxytocin infusion starting at 1-2 mU/min, titrated every 30 minutes to achieve 4-5 contractions per 10 minutes
new_options.rationale: Membranes are already ruptured and there is inadequate contraction frequency/strength causing slow progress in the active first stage. Oxytocin augmentation at 1-2 mU/min titrated every 30 minutes is the correct Australian protocol; faster titration risks hyperstimulation (RANZCOG; eTG).

new_options.text: Perform ARM and reassess in 4 hours before considering oxytocin
new_options.rationale: Membranes are already spontaneously ruptured; ARM is not applicable here (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Proceed to category 2 caesarean section for failure to progress
new_options.rationale: Failure to progress requires demonstration after adequate uterine activity (after oxytocin); proceeding to caesarean before oxytocin is premature (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Commence IV oxytocin infusion starting at 6 mU/min with no titration interval shorter than 60 minutes
new_options.rationale: Starting too high risks hyperstimulation and foetal compromise; standard Australian regimen starts low (1-2 mU/min) and titrates every 30 minutes (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Site an epidural and re-examine her in 6 hours without commencing any augmentation now
new_options.rationale: Pain relief is reasonable but does not address slow progress with inadequate contractions; delay risks foetal compromise (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 at 39+4 having a trial of labour after caesarean (one previous LSCS for breech) is in active labour at 7 cm with epidural. The CTG develops a sudden prolonged deceleration to 60 bpm, not recovering after 4 minutes despite stopping oxytocin, left lateral, 500 mL crystalloid and exclusion of hypotension. Fresh PV bleeding develops, scar tenderness, and the previously palpable head becomes ballotable abdominally (suspected uterine rupture). The registrar calls a category 1 caesarean section.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination most accurately describes the standards for category 1 caesarean and the immediate priorities?

new_options.text: Decision-to-delivery interval target 30 minutes; concurrent MTP activation, theatre transfer, anaesthetic preparation; do not delay for re-examination
new_options.rationale: The Australian/NICE standard for category 1 caesarean is delivery as soon as possible, with a 30-minute auditable threshold; bypassing further bedside investigation is appropriate when uterine rupture is suspected, and parallel resuscitation and MTP activation are essential (RANZCOG; RCOG).

new_options.text: Decision-to-delivery 15 minutes; defer MTP activation until intraoperative blood loss is confirmed
new_options.rationale: 15 minutes is an idealised target rarely achieved; the audited standard is 30 minutes. MTP should be activated proactively given suspected rupture (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Decision-to-delivery 60 minutes; obtain confirmatory bedside ultrasound before transfer to theatre
new_options.rationale: 60 minutes is the threshold for category 2 caesarean; rupture is a category 1 emergency and bedside imaging delays delivery (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Decision-to-delivery 75 minutes if epidural is already sited and theatre is in-house
new_options.rationale: Epidural availability does not change category-1 timing; the target is 30 minutes (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Decision-to-delivery 45 minutes; abandon TOLAC pathway only after delivery is achieved
new_options.rationale: TOLAC is abandoned at the moment rupture is suspected, not after delivery; target is 30 minutes (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 33-year-old G1P0 at 41+5 is undergoing induction of labour for prolonged pregnancy. She has received two doses of dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel 6 hours apart, the second 8 hours ago. Cervix today: 1 cm long, posterior, firm, 1 cm dilated, head at -3 (Bishop score 3). No regular contractions. CTG reassuring, membranes intact. EFW 3.4 kg, no contraindications to mechanical methods, no previous caesarean, no antepartum haemorrhage, no foetal compromise. Local protocol permits maximum 2 doses of dinoprostone gel in 24 hours and prefers mechanical ripening when prostaglandins fail. She has no allergies.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Insert a transcervical Foley catheter (16 Fr) with balloon inflated to 30-60 mL for up to 24 hours for mechanical cervical ripening
new_options.rationale: After failure of prostaglandins at maximum local dose with an unfavourable cervix, mechanical methods (Foley catheter or balloon) are the recommended next step. They are safe, effective and avoid hyperstimulation; ARM with oxytocin is inappropriate at Bishop 3 (RANZCOG IOL; NICE).

new_options.text: Administer a third dose of dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel today
new_options.rationale: Local protocol permits maximum 2 doses in 24 hours; exceeding this risks hyperstimulation (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Perform artificial rupture of membranes and commence IV oxytocin infusion
new_options.rationale: ARM at Bishop 3 with a high station is technically difficult and carries cord prolapse risk; cervix must first be ripened (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Perform a membrane sweep at the bedside and reassess in 24 hours
new_options.rationale: Membrane sweep is for low-Bishop cervices that are at least 1 cm dilated, but as a single intervention is insufficient after failed prostaglandins; mechanical ripening is preferred (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Proceed directly to category 4 elective caesarean section for failed induction of labour
new_options.rationale: Caesarean is reserved for genuine failed induction after both pharmacological and mechanical methods; she has not yet failed mechanical (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 38-year-old G5P4 has had a postpartum haemorrhage after spontaneous vaginal birth of a 4.2 kg neonate following oxytocin augmentation. Despite 40 IU oxytocin infusion, IM ergometrine, three doses IM carboprost, IV tranexamic acid 1 g, and a Bakri balloon at 500 mL, she continues to bleed. Total EBL 3500 mL. BP 88/52, HR 134, peripheries cool. Fibrinogen 1.4 g/L, platelets 68. MTP active, packed cells/FFP/platelets running. She has completed her family. Consultant obstetrician is at the bedside, gynae-oncology is in-house, interventional radiology is on site, anaesthetic team is in theatre.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Proceed to laparotomy with uterine-sparing measures first (B-Lynch compression suture +/- stepwise uterine artery ligation), escalating to peripartum hysterectomy if bleeding persists
new_options.rationale: RANZCOG PPH algorithm: after failure of medical therapy and balloon tamponade, the recommended surgical sequence is conservative interventions (B-Lynch compression, uterine artery ligation) before hysterectomy. She has completed her family so hysterectomy is acceptable if conservative steps fail, but should not be the first surgical step (RANZCOG PPH).

new_options.text: Proceed directly to peripartum hysterectomy without trialling uterine-sparing surgery
new_options.rationale: Hysterectomy is the last resort even when family is complete; conservative surgical measures are recommended first if patient remains stabilisable (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Insert a second balloon alongside the Bakri and arrange transfer to interventional radiology for embolisation
new_options.rationale: She is haemodynamically unstable; transfer to IR outside theatre is unsafe. IR embolisation is reserved for the haemodynamically stable patient (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Re-inflate Bakri to 750 mL and observe for 30 minutes before further intervention
new_options.rationale: Bakri has a maximum recommended fill (~500 mL); overinflation does not increase efficacy. Continued observation in shock is unsafe (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Bilateral internal iliac artery ligation as the first surgical step
new_options.rationale: Internal iliac artery ligation is technically demanding, takes time, and is reserved for after uterine artery ligation or by IR/vascular if available; B-Lynch is the standard first conservative step (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 27-year-old primipara, 6 weeks postpartum, exclusively breastfeeding her thriving term infant, attends with a 14-hour history of a discrete tender lump in the lower inner right breast. Overlying skin has faint pink mottling but no defined erythema. T 36.8, no rigors, no flu-like symptoms, infant feeding effectively. Recent change: baby has slept a longer 5-hour stretch the past two nights. No fluctuance, no axillary adenopathy. She has read online that 'aggressive deep massage' and 'pump-and-dump' are recommended.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

new_options.text: Continue feeding from both breasts on demand, apply cold packs between feeds, ibuprofen for pain, and review in 24 hours; avoid deep massage and pump-and-dump
new_options.rationale: The Academy of Breastfeeding Medicine 2022 protocol describes this as ductal narrowing/inflammatory mastitis spectrum, not bacterial mastitis. Management is on-demand feeding, cold packs, NSAIDs, and AVOIDANCE of deep massage and pumping (both can worsen inflammation/oedema). Antibiotics are reserved for bacterial mastitis (RANZCOG; ABM 2022).

new_options.text: Commence oral flucloxacillin 500 mg qid for 7 days now
new_options.rationale: There is no fever, systemic symptoms or defined erythema. Antibiotics are not indicated for inflammatory mastitis/ductal narrowing and may worsen dysbiosis (ABM 2022).

new_options.text: Aggressive deep breast massage every 2 hours with hot compresses for 48 hours
new_options.rationale: Aggressive massage worsens tissue oedema and risks abscess; this is the major anti-pattern that ABM 2022 specifically warns against (ABM 2022).

new_options.text: Cease feeding from the affected breast and pump-and-dump for 48 hours
new_options.rationale: Cessation of feeding worsens engorgement and the inflammatory cycle; on-demand feeding remains essential (ABM 2022).

new_options.text: Urgent breast ultrasound and surgical referral for incision and drainage
new_options.rationale: There is no fluctuance, abscess features or failure of conservative management; surgical referral is premature (ABM 2022).

verdicts.discard_reason: Engorgement vs mastitis discrimination duplicates the lever already tested in revised obgyn-lact-uro-002 (inflammatory mastitis anti-pattern). One lactation-spectrum L3 question is sufficient; engorgement alone is recall-level.

verdicts.new_stem: A 58-year-old G3P3 postmenopausal woman presents with 6 months of a vaginal bulge worse at end of day and a need to splint to defaecate. She also reports new-onset stress urinary incontinence on coughing, urgency without leakage, and one episode of incomplete bladder emptying requiring her to bear down. No faecal incontinence, no dyspareunia. BMI 26, postmenopausal 8 years. Speculum and POP-Q: Ba 0 (anterior at hymen on Valsalva), Bp +1 (posterior beyond hymen), C -5 (cervix well supported), Aa 0, Ap +1. Post-void residual 90 mL. Bowel motions Bristol 4 on fibre.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which compartment defect best explains her splinting-to-defaecate symptom specifically?

new_options.text: Posterior vaginal compartment defect (rectocele), evidenced by Bp +1 and need to splint posteriorly to evacuate stool
new_options.rationale: Splinting the posterior vagina to evacuate stool is the classic symptom of rectocele (posterior compartment); Bp +1 confirms posterior wall descent. The cystocele (Ba 0) explains her urinary symptoms, but the defaecatory symptom is rectocele-specific (RANZCOG; IUGA).

new_options.text: Anterior vaginal compartment defect (cystocele), causing her splinting symptom
new_options.rationale: Cystocele causes urinary splinting (anterior wall splinting to void) not posterior splinting to defaecate; she also has a cystocele but it does not explain this specific symptom (IUGA).

new_options.text: Apical compartment defect of the uterine cervix, causing her splinting symptom
new_options.rationale: Apical descent typically presents as dragging/heaviness; C -5 here is well-supported, ruling out apical prolapse (IUGA).

new_options.text: Enterocele of the pouch of Douglas, causing her splinting symptom
new_options.rationale: Enterocele may coexist but requires specific clinical or radiological identification; her examination is more consistent with rectocele than enterocele (IUGA).

new_options.text: Post-hysterectomy vault prolapse defect
new_options.rationale: She is G3P3 with intact cervix on examination (C -5); she has not had a hysterectomy so vault prolapse is not applicable (IUGA).

verdicts.new_stem: A 28-year-old nulliparous woman presents with a 3-day history of progressively painful right vulval swelling, now interfering with walking. T 37.1, no rigors, no STI symptoms. Examination: 4 cm fluctuant tender mass at the right posterolateral introitus (5 o'clock) consistent with a Bartholin abscess; intact non-necrotic skin, no cellulitis spreading beyond. This is her THIRD Bartholin abscess at the same site in the last 12 months. The two previous episodes were treated with simple incision and drainage with packing, and both recurred within 3 months. She is otherwise well, on no medications, and wants definitive treatment that minimises further recurrence.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management?

new_options.text: Simple incision and drainage with packing under local anaesthetic
new_options.rationale: Simple I&D with packing has high recurrence rates (this is her third recurrence); a definitive procedure is now needed (RANZCOG; eTG).

new_options.text: Marsupialisation under general anaesthetic
new_options.rationale: After recurrent Bartholin abscesses at the same site despite Word catheter or I&D, marsupialisation provides definitive long-term drainage with lower recurrence than I&D and is preferred over excision (which is reserved for failed marsupialisation or suspicion of malignancy) (RANZCOG; eTG).

new_options.text: Word catheter insertion under local anaesthetic for 4-6 weeks
new_options.rationale: Word catheter is an acceptable first-line option for a primary or single recurrence; with three recurrences at the same site, more definitive marsupialisation is recommended (RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 7 days without drainage
new_options.rationale: An abscess requires drainage; antibiotics alone do not resolve it. Antibiotics are reserved for surrounding cellulitis or sepsis (eTG; RANZCOG).

new_options.text: Excision of the Bartholin gland as primary management
new_options.rationale: Excision is reserved for failed marsupialisation or where malignancy is suspected (usually in women >40); it has higher morbidity (RANZCOG).

verdicts.new_stem: A 29-year-old G2P2 attends her 6-week postnatal review. She delivered vaginally and is exclusively breastfeeding on demand, day and night, no formula or solids. She has had no return of menstruation since delivery, and a urine beta-hCG is negative today. She and her partner do not wish to conceive again for at least 2 years. She has heard friends say 'breastfeeding stops periods so I don't need contraception' and asks whether that is true. She has no UKMEC category 3-4 contraindications.
verdicts.new_lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice and recommendation?

new_options.text: Counsel that LAM provides about 98% efficacy only up to 6 months post partum and only while she is amenorrhoeic and exclusively/near-exclusively breastfeeding; offer a parallel reliable method (LARC or POP) to avoid the gap when LAM expires
new_options.rationale: LAM is reliable only while ALL three criteria are met (exclusive/near-exclusive breastfeeding, infant under 6 months, amenorrhoea). At 6 weeks she meets criteria, but planning for the transition is part of good counselling: parallel LARC or progestogen-only methods bridge the gap (FSRH; Family Planning Australia).

new_options.text: Reassure that breastfeeding alone provides reliable contraception for up to 12 months post partum
new_options.rationale: LAM is reliable only up to 6 months, not 12; failure rate rises sharply once any criterion lapses (FSRH).

new_options.text: Reassure that any breastfeeding plus amenorrhoea provides reliable contraception regardless of feeding pattern or solids
new_options.rationale: Partial or mixed feeding (with formula or solids) breaks LAM criteria; reliability drops markedly (Family Planning Australia).

new_options.text: Counsel that LAM is reliable until 6 months only if one normal menstrual period has occurred to confirm cycles have restarted
new_options.rationale: Return of menstruation INVALIDATES LAM; the criterion is amenorrhoea since delivery, not the presence of menses (Family Planning Australia).

new_options.text: Recommend commencing the combined oral contraceptive pill today as the most reliable bridging method
new_options.rationale: CHC is UKMEC 4 in the first 6 weeks postpartum if breastfeeding (UKMEC 2 from 6 weeks to 6 months) due to VTE and reduced milk supply; progestogen-only methods or LARCs are preferred (UKMEC 2016).

verdicts.new_stem: A 28-year-old G1P1 exclusively breastfeeding her 3-week-old infant presents with a 36-hour history of a painful hot wedge of erythema in the upper outer right breast, fever 38.4, rigors, and infant feeding poorly from the affected side. Examination: 6 cm wedge erythema, warm, tender, NO fluctuance. Bedside USS shows no abscess. She has continued frequent feeding and warm compresses without improvement. Importantly, she has a documented IgE-mediated immediate hypersensitivity to penicillin (rash, urticaria and lip swelling 30 minutes after amoxicillin 5 years ago, confirmed by allergy clinic).
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line oral antibiotic regimen?

new_options.text: Flucloxacillin 500 mg orally every 6 hours for 5 days
new_options.rationale: Flucloxacillin is a penicillin and contraindicated in IgE-mediated penicillin allergy (eTG).

new_options.text: Cefalexin 500 mg orally every 6 hours for 5 days
new_options.rationale: Cephalosporins carry a small (~1-2%) cross-reactivity risk in immediate-type penicillin allergy and should be avoided in confirmed anaphylactic-type history; eTG recommends clindamycin in this setting (eTG).

new_options.text: Clindamycin 450 mg orally every 8 hours for 5-10 days
new_options.rationale: For bacterial mastitis in a woman with immediate-type penicillin hypersensitivity, Therapeutic Guidelines recommend clindamycin as the first-line oral alternative; it is compatible with breastfeeding (eTG; ABM 2022).

new_options.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg orally every 12 hours for 7 days
new_options.rationale: TMP-SMX is contraindicated in infants under 2 months (risk of kernicterus); at 3 weeks it is best avoided, and it has poor S. aureus coverage as first-line (eTG).

new_options.text: Continue conservative measures alone for a further 48 hours
new_options.rationale: She has clear bacterial mastitis (wedge erythema, fever 38.4, rigors) that has not responded to 36 hours of conservative care; antibiotics are required (ABM 2022).

verdicts.new_stem: A 28-year-old G2P2 on day 2 post-vaginal-birth (PPH 1100 mL controlled with uterotonics and tranexamic acid; no transfusion) feels tired but not breathless, ambulates without symptoms, breastfeeds well. Booking Hb 124 g/L. Today: Hb 78 g/L, MCV 76, ferritin 12 mcg/L, transferrin saturation 9%, platelets 240, normal renal function. No active bleeding, no chest pain, no syncope. She wishes to be discharged tomorrow to care for her toddler. Local protocol follows the National Blood Authority Patient Blood Management Module 5 (Obstetrics).
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management of her postpartum iron deficiency anaemia?

new_options.text: Single dose IV ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg before discharge with review at 4 weeks
new_options.rationale: Hb 70-80 g/L in a stable postpartum woman with no ongoing bleeding does not mandate transfusion (PBM Module 5: transfusion thresholds Hb <70 g/L in stable patients). IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) corrects iron stores rapidly, supports breastfeeding and avoids transfusion risks (NBA PBM5; AMH).
source_refs: National Blood Authority PBM Module 5 - Obstetrics and Maternity

new_options.text: Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells before discharge
new_options.rationale: She is asymptomatic at rest with stable haemodynamics and no ongoing bleeding; transfusion is not indicated above the restrictive Hb 70 g/L threshold (NBA PBM5).
source_refs: National Blood Authority PBM Module 5 - Obstetrics and Maternity

new_options.text: Oral ferrous sulphate 325 mg three times daily for 3 months
new_options.rationale: Oral iron at this Hb level corrects slowly (months) and is poorly tolerated; IV iron is the recommended preferred option for postpartum severe iron deficiency (NBA PBM5).
source_refs: National Blood Authority PBM Module 5 - Obstetrics and Maternity

new_options.text: Oral ferrous fumarate 200 mg three times daily with no scheduled follow-up
new_options.rationale: Oral iron alone for severe deficiency is suboptimal, and follow-up is required to confirm correction (NBA PBM5).
source_refs: National Blood Authority PBM Module 5 - Obstetrics and Maternity

new_options.text: Erythropoietin-stimulating agent subcutaneously now plus oral iron for 3 months
new_options.rationale: ESAs are not routinely indicated in postpartum iron-deficiency anaemia without renal disease; IV iron alone usually suffices (NBA PBM5; AMH).
source_refs: National Blood Authority PBM Module 5 - Obstetrics and Maternity

verdicts.discard_reason: Cervical screening timing is straightforward recall after the recent NCSP changes are known; the bank already has three NCSP questions (obgyn-006, obgyn-gynae-hy-014, obgyn-gynonc-002). A fourth would be redundant.

verdicts.new_stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 with booking BMI 27, no diabetes history, no PCOS, no previous GDM has a 75 g OGTT at 27 weeks: fasting 5.3, 1 h 8.4, 2 h 7.1 mmol/L. The midwife asks whether this meets Australian (ADIPS) criteria for gestational diabetes, and if so, what the immediate next step is. The woman has no symptoms and her foetus is growing on the 50th centile.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which interpretation and next step are correct?

new_options.text: GDM diagnosed on the fasting >=5.1 mmol/L cut-off; commence dietitian and diabetes educator referral, capillary glucose monitoring 4 times daily, and review at the joint diabetes-in-pregnancy clinic within 1 week
new_options.rationale: ADIPS 2014 criteria: ANY of fasting >=5.1, 1 h >=10.0, 2 h >=8.5 mmol/L. Her fasting 5.3 meets the threshold; even though the 1 h and 2 h values are below cut-off, GDM is diagnosed. Initial management is multidisciplinary team referral with SMBG (RANZCOG; ADIPS).

new_options.text: Not GDM; only the fasting result is borderline; repeat OGTT in 4 weeks
new_options.rationale: ADIPS criteria require only ONE elevated value to diagnose GDM; her fasting 5.3 already meets the threshold (ADIPS).

new_options.text: GDM only if fasting >=5.5 mmol/L; her result is below threshold
new_options.rationale: The Australian fasting cut-off is 5.1 mmol/L, not 5.5 (ADIPS).

new_options.text: Type 2 diabetes diagnosed (fasting >=7.0); commence insulin today
new_options.rationale: Diabetes outside pregnancy uses fasting >=7.0; in pregnancy the GDM cut-off is 5.1. She has GDM, not overt T2DM (ADIPS).

new_options.text: Borderline only; reassure with healthy-eating advice and re-test at 32 weeks
new_options.rationale: She meets diagnostic criteria; deferral risks foetal complications (macrosomia, stillbirth) (ADIPS).
